original_abstract:
Feline immunodeficiency virus (FIV) and feline leukaemia virus (FeLV) represent significant global threats to feline health, negatively impacting the clinical well-being and longevity of affected animals. Despite their clinical importance, comprehensive data regarding the prevalence of these retroviruses in Portuguese shelter environments has remained limited. This study aimed to estimate the seroprevalence of FIV and FeLV among stray cats admitted to a shelter in northeast Portugal. Blood samples were collected from 326 cats during standardized medical intake examinations between 2017 and 2023. Screening was performed using a point-of-care immunochromatographic assay to detect FIV antibodies (gp40) and FeLV antigens (p27).

The results revealed an overall retroviral seroprevalence of 15.3% (50/326). Specifically, 9.8% of the cats were positive for FIV alone, 2.1% for FeLV alone, and 3.4% were co-infected with both viruses. Statistical analysis indicated that adult cats had a significantly higher prevalence of FIV and co-infections compared to younger cats. However, other demographic factors—including sex, breed, hair length, and the specific municipality of origin—did not show a significant association with infection status. These findings provide the first detailed assessment of retroviral distribution in Portugal’s shelter population. The data underscores high viral circulation among stray cats and highlights an urgent need for rigorous screening at shelter entry, increased adherence to vaccination protocols, and the implementation of robust management strategies to mitigate the spread of these pathogens within feline communities.
humanized_abstract:
Feline immunodeficiency virus (FIV) and feline leukaemia virus (FeLV) are major global factors affecting the health and survival of infected cats and affecting their clinical health and life span. Information regarding FIV and FeLV prevalence in shelter cats in Portugal is lacking. Therefore, the aim of this study was to estimate the FIV and FeLV seroprevalence in a population of stray cats admitted to a shelter located in the northeast of Portugal. Blood samples were collected from 326 cats following a complete medical checkup at the shelter, during the period from 2017 to 2023. Cats were tested for FIV (gp40 antibodies) and FeLV (p27 antigens) infection using a rapid point-of-care immunochromatographic test.

Our study determined the prevalence of retroviruses (FIV, FeLV) in shelter cats in Portugal. Serum samples from 326 cats were analysed for the presence of antibodies against these viruses. The overall retroviral seroprevalence was 15.3% (50/326) with 9.8% (32/326) and 2.1% (7/326) being FIV and FeLV positive, respectively, while 3.4% (11/326) were co-infected with both viruses. Adult animals were more frequently infected with FIV and retrovirus combinations. No association was, however, demonstrated for the other investigated factors, i.e. sex, breed, hair length and the municipality of origin. This study represents the first in-depth investigation of the prevalence of retroviruses among shelter cats in Portugal. It suggests high viral load in the feline population in this country and thus the necessity to screen all cats admitted to shelters more frequently, to improve the vaccination regime of the animals in shelters, and to implement control measures to prevent the spread of the viruses in the feline populations.
